Typed-data-tree vocabulary: branches and leaves. The workspace's typed records (structs and enums in the sema database) form trees of branches and leaves. A LEAF is a fixed-size terminal — typically an enum of unit variants only (Bool as True/False; Magnitude's 7 unit variants; Kind's five-kind taxonomy). Final leaves have no variable size. A BRANCH carries variable-size or data-carrying content — vectors, data-carrying enum variants, structs containing variable-size members. Branches are themselves trees (recursive). A vector is a kind of dynamic-leaf in one reading (single field at one position) but practically a branch because it points into another tree or series of trees. A data-carrying enum variant is a branch because the variant's payload doesn't fit as a single tree node. Adopt this vocabulary going forward in design conversations and skill files.

In an active troubleshooting task where the user asked for a working connection, the agent should not stop at diagnosis when the next step is a narrow safe fix; it should apply the fix and then report the result.

A type's kind is announced by the delimiter that wraps its value, the same way a brace body means struct and a square-bracket body means enum. Generics use the reserved pipe-parenthesis delimiter (| ... |), pipes inside, mirroring the existing pipe-text string delimiter. A generic declaration is Name (| [params] body |): the pipe-parenthesis marks the type generic, the params are the binder list, and the body is an ordinary struct or enum body nested inside. This assigns meaning to one of the two reserved pipe-bracket extension points named by j9du; nota-next already parses these blocks (PipeParenthesis and PipeBrace delimiters exist), so it is schema-level work, not a seed-parser change. Realizes j9du and satisfies 3742.

Enum variants cannot be declared without an enum type name. A parenthesized list such as ((Parse Expression) (Render Expression)) has no enum type name for the outer list, so it is not a valid enum declaration surface. Any enum declaration must name the enum type; variant tags such as Parse and Render only make sense inside that named enum declaration.

Known-root NOTA serialization must not be implemented as ad hoc field string joins on each schema object. A file read as a known root type is already the root body; the NOTA layer should expose an object/body parsing and encoding abstraction that reads and writes the ordered fields of the known type.

After NOTA structural parsing matches a file body or a delimited object, the next semantic parsing step should receive the matched body's inner object stream rather than the outer delimiter wrapper. Known-root files and matched delimited objects share the same body abstraction: the expected type decides whether the body is read as a struct, vector, enum variant, or other value.

NOTA's own grammar can be schema-described, making the notation self-hosting. Its delimiter set is a closed enum: the three base pairs parenthesis, square-bracket, brace, plus three piped variants pipe-parenthesis, pipe-text, and pipe-brace. The pipe-text delimiter is already defined and used by NOTA for bracket-safe and multiline strings. The pipe-parenthesis and pipe-brace delimiters exist in the grammar but carry no assigned meaning, and are reserved as extension points for extended NOTA languages such as schema to define their own constructs. Schema-ing NOTA closes the self-describing loop: schema describes the contracts, the structural macro nodes, and NOTA itself.

NOTA is a thin library for sense-of-structure - it exposes methods on parsed blocks (is_square_bracket / is_parenthesis / is_brace / holds_N_objects / qualifies_as_symbol / etc) that are macro primitives. Criome's system direction is a universal agreement machine for authorization. The pulse is an object-update pulse: when a content-addressed object or authorized state transition is admitted, criome emits an authorized-object reference and the router, as the sole operational matcher for all non-direct message passing, matches component subscriptions and fans the reference out to the affected components. Criome emits references, not object payloads; components fetch or request the referenced rkyv object through the routing/object-distribution layer. Normal accepted objects are quorum-backed, not single-node signed: a lone node signature has little value except narrow bootstrap or diagnostic cases, so the practical design default is quorum authorization for meaningful objects and trust-crossing state. Time-driven pulses are not an ambient global heartbeat. A heartbeat is programmed by accepting a contract with an after-time condition: that acceptance schedules a later check of the contract against related events; when the crystallized time condition matures, criome checks whether the relevant events happened, and if they did not, it triggers a new acceptance for the time-based condition's resulting state, to be quorum-signed. Criome keeps no operational delivery registry; any criome-local subscription surface is observation and audit only, with the direct criome-to-criome agreement lane the sole non-router message path.

Short-header packing optimization. The 8 sub-enums default to one byte each (256 variants per slot) but can be smaller or larger. Small sub-enums fit in fewer bits — Bool fits in 1 bit (Bool is an enum of two variants). A sub-enum with 16 variants fits in 4 bits, freeing 4 bits at that byte position for other use. Multi-byte sub-enums are allowed when one sub-enum needs more than 256 variants — combine two or more byte positions for that enum. Freed bits can encode deeper variant nesting (the name of more inner variants), giving the header more discriminator depth without growing past 64 bits. Integers are NOT valid sub-enum kinds — they are typed values not discrete variants; only enums (including Bool) fit. *encoding*

A struct field's role is its type, so no struct has two fields of the same type. Distinct roles are distinct types, giving dimensional correctness: Height and Width are both metres yet cannot be interchanged or multiplied as like. Repetition is a keyed collection, never repeated fields, since a Vector is a Map over an ordinal index. Field-name equals type-name is the default, and an explicit field name signals a missing type or a collection. Full type-level enforcement is aspirational. The principle guides schema and nota design now.

The criome daemon holds an encrypted key store and can hold more than one key - sub-keys under the store. It always has a self-start key, the only key that must be readable by the daemon itself and therefore by the owning user: the user home private key representing his home, encrypted at rest with the home-user or system encryption. The other keys are sub-encrypted within the store. If the owner suspects a leak he can rotate; a more secure hardware-based key mechanism is a later refinement. This extends psc6 from a single generated master BLS key to a managed multi-key store.

criome's authorization verdict can be supplied by a connected approver, not only by gathered BLS quorum signatures. Client approval mode is a daemon-wide AuthorizationMode variant alongside Quorum and AutoApprove: criome parks every incoming submission for human approval rather than auto-authorizing or gathering a quorum. criome owns the pending-approval queue — each parked AuthorizationEvaluation lives in criome's own store keyed by a ParkedAuthorizationId, mentci lists and observes the parked submissions over criome's meta socket and casts the approve-or-deny verdict by that identifier rather than re-supplying the full evaluation by value; this is the vote-on-existing-object adjudication where mentci approves or rejects the already-submitted, criome-held object. criome also supports a configured auto-approve acceptance policy that authorizes requests as they arrive, for bootstrap and testing. All are verdict sources inside criome's verify-and-record model; gathered-signature quorum stays the production multi-party path.

Schema is the composability and programmability layer on top of NOTA: it turns NOTA into a system for describing composable data types and data-driven behavior, programmable by the user to create new interfaces for specialized engine behavior expressed as a node-graph data type. Schemas nest: an engine may contain sub-engines, each with its own schema and interface, so signal, nexus, and sema are the three top schemas but internal sub-behaviors get their own. The namespace mirrors Rust modules: one file with fully-qualified colon-separated names, or split into files like Rust modules, importable and re-exportable. A schema is a key-value struct at the top whose entries declare the module public symbols. Imports are a key-value map of module name to a data-carrying variant naming the import KIND such as import-all, from-git, from-path, adjacent-directory, same-directory, or from-repository-root, so the import system is modular across scenarios.

rkyv's natural storage layout uses a larger namespace than the data strictly needs (a bool occupies a byte; a small-variant enum's discriminator fits within a byte even when there are only 2-3 active variants). This headroom enables a class of zero-cost schema changes that require no data migration whatsoever: adding a fourth unit variant to a three-variant enum that still fits in the byte. Constraint: variant order must be preserved (existing payload bytes must still decode under the new schema). The schema-layout schema must expose this headroom (e.g., declared discriminator width) so the migration-derivation step can identify zero-cost changes and skip the migration step. Other zero-cost-class candidates: adding fields at the end of a struct under append-only encoding; widening fixed-width integer fields where rkyv reserves space.

Single-object sigil sugar is an open macro-node design frontier: a leading sigil can eventually make one NOTA object stand for a larger pair or field expression when the sigil makes the intended macro case obvious. This is exploratory and must be represented as macro-node configuration/data rather than parser magic.

The schema-crystallized architectural principles (the records captured in this turn: schema crystallizes architecture; extensible header pattern; deep tree of enum-struct with type index; two languages internal/external; interact trait and interaction actor mediation; effect table mapping; actor fan-out execution) are LOAD-BEARING for all subsequent architecture and intent work. Actor execution produces FAN-OUT outputs. When an interaction actor decides an outcome, it emits multiple things in parallel: execution effects, responses returned to the originating actors, AND additional messages dispatched to other actors (typical: send a mutation message to the database actor, send a notification message to the subscription actor, return a reply to the requester - all from one interaction-actor decision). Single-input-multiple-output is the normal shape; the actor never produces just one thing. The schema encodes the fan-out outputs declaratively - per interact-trait variant, the set of possible outputs is fixed and closed.

The update mechanism (sema-upgrade) is a structural prerequisite for any deployed persona component. The deploy → restart → update flow means a daemon coming up against an existing redb must either find no schema drift (no migration needed) or have a migration path through sema-upgrade. Without that path, any contract edit after first deploy breaks the next restart. The implication for getting mind + orchestrate deployed: their schema must be stable enough OR sema-upgrade must cover their migration before deployment, or the operator must be willing to drop-and-rebuild on every contract edit until sema-upgrade catches them.

Criome's internal language is a limited typed policy language over public-key identity atoms - NOT a general-purpose virtual machine - drawing its limited-operation discipline from the constrained VMs of Ethereum, Tezos, and Solana. Public keys are the atomic unit of identity; above them it composes complex identity contracts from signature and time-lock mechanics: signature quorums of k-of-n form and thresholds that increase or decrease over elapsed time. It carries explicit divergence-reconciliation objects for when two networks split and how the reconciliation is determined, where conflict resolution may be mediated by an LLM-oracle call to a provider which itself resolves through one of those identity contracts - for example a paid expert panel adjudicating the fairest resolution model. This is criome's Nexus object and verb vocabulary, the objects and operations the identity world treats, to be researched, defined in schema, and prototyped.

This follows the existing two-contract triad shape (two authority surfaces, two sockets, two listener actors) and supersedes the single cloud schema target in cloud ARCHITECTURE.md. Schema language has a lowering executor — a recursive parser walks the NOTA schema tree and dispatches at each node based on bracket/paren shape, lowering the transient sugar form into a fully-specced canonical form. The dispatch rules: bare identifier = reference to namespace entry (resolved by lookup); square bracket vector = enum declaration with variants; single-paren single-identifier = newtype tuple wrapping the named type; multi-element parens = struct with positional fields; (fieldname [variants]) = inline enum field with explicit name. The executor assembles the full schema by resolving paths and references.
Schema headers are enum namespace nodes, not one payload pair per verb.

The basic schema-emitted type definitions should derive NOTA traits and rkyv traits from the same data objects. Additional communication traits belong on the generated schema root objects when those objects cross component boundaries.

Root struct positional fields are bare — no Name@ prefix. The universal Name@Delimiter rule (record 1216) applies to DECLARATIONS where the user invents a new type name, not to positional values at the root struct's known fields. A .schema file's root is a struct with fixed positional fields (imports / input / output / namespace per the schema-of-schemas); the value at each field is bare — empty-brace imports, [Record@Entry Observe@Query ...] input enum variants, [RecordAccepted@... ...] output variants, brace-namespace declarations. Same treatment bare {} for imports and bare { ... } for the namespace already had; Input/Output need the same treatment because they are the same kind of thing — values at positional root-struct slots, not user declarations. Additional plane roots (NexusInput, NexusOutput, SemaInput, SemaOutput) declared INSIDE the namespace stay as Name@[...] because they ARE user declarations. Corrects the 1216 carve-out that named Input, Output, and plane roots together as needing Name@: top-level Input/Output are positional values, not declarations. Per psyche 2026-05-30 against report 429 §5a.

A single string that qualifies as a symbol name must be encoded in NOTA as a bare symbol, not quoted with brackets. Bracket strings are not the representation for schema names that are symbol-safe.

input and output are PARTITIONS of a single enum tag space - not two separate independent enums but two halves of one tag namespace at the wire level; the conceptual split for a 1-byte enum carrying ~240 slots is approximately two-thirds: first ~120 slots reserved for INPUT variants, slots ~121 to ~240 reserved for OUTPUT variants; this means at the wire byte level a single tag byte identifies BOTH the variant and whether the message is an input or output by which range it falls in; the schema author declares Input and Output separately at the schema level but the rkyv-compiled binary form merges them into one tag space; this is also the conceptual basis for the structural header at the message layer - first byte tells you immediately whether to dispatch on input handlers or output handlers; the specific 240-slot 120-120 split is illustrative not locked - larger enums get larger spaces *encoding*

Three-tier signal type sizing: (1) 64-bit micro-message — the default auto-loggable variant every Signal Operation/Reply/Effect/SemaObservation implements via macro, packing root variant tag plus packed sub-discriminators into a u64; (2) 64-byte summary message — hand-implemented when a type has natural summary fields (public key + metadata, short string, etc.) — basis for a second subscription tier; (3) full unrestricted message — current rkyv-encoded records. Variant always at the root. Macro autogens tier 1 trait. Research needed on what fits in 64 bits and 64 bytes plus storage/logging efficiency

Nexus input and Nexus output must not be modeled as symmetric lists of the same operation categories. Nexus input is what Nexus receives as facts/replies/events to decide from; Nexus output is what Nexus commands or emits next. If both sides list Signal and SEMA operations in the same-looking way, the interface loses meaning and reads as nonsense.

The signal/nexus/sema-bearing schema should be called a REACTIVE SCHEMA. Analogy to Nix: derivation is the built-in primitive, and higher-level builders extend it; likewise the plain SCHEMA is the primitive that specifies data types (the shapes), and the REACTIVE SCHEMA is the extension that adds the reaction surface (signal in, nexus through, sema state, output back). Plain schema says what data IS; reactive schema says how data MOVES. Most schemas are plain; a component top-level schema is reactive. Naming proposed by the psyche; pending the schema-syntax design conversation settling.

There is no composite intent type in code - composite and agglomeration are a language and behavior concept for expressing an idea, not a record type or variant. This refines the earlier composite-record direction (Principle audg3): provenance and agglomeration are expressed through a relations field on records, not a dedicated Composite type. Refreshing several intents need not produce one merged record - it may produce two or three records of different kinds.

Signal root-level macro composition emits 64-bit super-tiny message headers. The 64 bits encode root type plus variant kind (data-carrying or unit) plus type info sufficient to discriminate any object in the namespace; object recognition is constant-time on the header. Every signal contract macro emits headers matching exactly this namespace shape.

Schema and Spirit should move toward a runtime-upgradable schema system: schema changes are represented as typed upgrade objects, and Rust generation emits upgrade and compatibility code from those objects so new generated components can accept previous schema/data/message shapes through explicit typed upgrade paths.

Future forge build logic may let generated Rust code become content-addressed crates directly, reducing the need for separate generated-code repositories once forge can own that build path outside Nix.

The schema component exposes a schema type library — types that other crates import to reference schema metadata. The library is the build-time substrate consumed by macros (the signal-side macro plus the symmetric sema-side macro per spirit 390). Three faces of the schema component: (1) Runtime daemon — serves short-header to schema lookups; (2) Library crate — exposes the schema types other crates import; (3) Macro substrate — the library is what the signal/sema macros consume when generating code from a NOTA schema input (per spirit 391). The library is the build-time face; the daemon is the runtime face; both stem from the same NOTA schema source.
Guardian model performs semantic judgment for every guarded write; deterministic code gathers context, validates structure, and applies typed verdict consequences. *admission control*
Schema source shape drops outer wrapper and uses header-first sections

Upgrade mechanism: (1) schema changes between versions; (2) schema-diff identifies what moved/changed/dropped/renamed; (3) hand-written Rust bridge code per version-boundary handles the moved or transformed data types; (4) version number marks the boundary - sets which schema applies; (5) the new daemon is recompiled with the previous schema also available for reference; (6) auto-migration on database load - daemon reads DB version marker; if DB is on previous (main, in next/main/previous vocab per rec 672) version, the migration method runs once, transforms all data to the new shape, updates the version marker, persists the new database. Assembled schemas can potentially be stored to enable efficient diffing.

Schema-emitted Signal, Nexus, and SEMA engines are kameo actors, not synchronous method spines driven by a runner loop over mutex-wrapped state. Actors everywhere is the architecture; the generated triad daemons are not exempt. Deferring advanced runtime machinery — backpressure, scheduling, the runtime-control trait surface — never meant deferring actors or emitting synchronous daemons. A minimal kameo actor carrying on_start and on_stop adopts kameo now while that deeper machinery stays deferred. The current synchronous daemon emission with mutex-wrapped engine state is drift to correct, not a sanctioned end state.
Provider integrations may be build-time opt-ins, and capability observation must distinguish built-but-unconfigured providers from providers not built into the daemon.

Agents may inspect authentication tokens and other secret values when the task requires plaintext access authorized by the psyche or by an existing workspace task. Visible plaintext is transient working material: keep it out of chat, reports, logs, commits, argv, shell traces, the Nix store, test fixtures, and checked-in files; prefer blind pipe-to-sink handling when it is enough; when inspection is needed, minimize exposure and move the value directly to the requested consumer or encrypted store.

The assembled schema tree is the complete input to Rust generation, including code to read and write NOTA text and portable rkyv binary data for SEMA and signal formats; Rust emission may later be invoked from a single macro that reads repository schema files.
Every engine should have an object that owns the logic it runs, with daemon startup and transport boilerplate pushed behind libraries or macros.

The aski-core (and its successor askic / askicc) pattern is an analog past experience, not the foundation to replicate. The earlier design intent there was a more beautiful syntax to represent Rust; the persona-component schema work is an entirely different goal. The new direction is a dedicated schema specification language akin to Cap'n Proto schema files but more explicit, scoped only to the workspace's Rust subset (no tuples, since the workspace treats tuples as poorly-defined structs). Reading aski-core and askicc as foundational substrate for design was the wrong frame; treat them as analogues that inform the discipline without dictating shape.
